annotations.label: Derivative (informal)

anchor.snippet: the derivative is a fundamental tool that quantifies the sensitivity to change

annotations.note: Mathlib's `fderiv` is the general (Fréchet) derivative on normed spaces; `deriv` specializes to scalar input.

annotations.label: Differentiability at a point (ε-δ)

anchor.snippet: is differentiable at a point

annotations.note: `DifferentiableAt 𝕜 f x` captures differentiability at a point via the Fréchet condition (which agrees with ε-δ on `ℝ`).

annotations.label: Derivative as a limit

anchor.snippet: then this limit is called the derivative of

annotations.note: `deriv f x` is defined via `fderiv`, but `HasDerivAt` is equivalent to the classical difference-quotient limit.

annotations.label: Derivative function

anchor.snippet: This function is written

annotations.note: `deriv f : 𝕜 → F` is exactly the derivative function viewed as a function of the point.

annotations.label: Derivative of squaring function

anchor.snippet: let [MATH] be the squaring function

annotations.note: `deriv_pow_field` gives `deriv (· ^ n) x = n * x^(n-1)`; specializing to `n = 2` yields the squaring example.

annotations.label: Derivative as slope of tangent

anchor.snippet: In other words, the derivative is the slope of the tangent

annotations.note: Mathlib has no geometric `tangentLine` concept identifying `deriv` with the slope of a tangent line (only the analytic `HasDerivAt`/`slope` machinery).

annotations.label: Derivative via infinitesimals (nonstandard analysis)

anchor.snippet: the derivative of [MATH] becomes

annotations.note: `Hyperreal` exists in `Mathlib.Analysis.Real.Hyperreal` but no derivative defined via `st` of an infinitesimal ratio is provided.

annotations.label: Squaring function via infinitesimals

anchor.snippet: Taking the squaring function

annotations.note: No nonstandard derivative is set up in Mathlib, so this worked example is not formalized.

annotations.label: Differentiability implies continuity

anchor.snippet: then [MATH] must also be continuous at

annotations.note: `DifferentiableAt.continuousAt` states that any function differentiable at `x` is continuous at `x`.

annotations.label: Step function is not differentiable

anchor.snippet: be the step function that returns the value 1

annotations.note: No dedicated lemma showing the Heaviside/step function is non-differentiable; one would derive it from the discontinuity, but no named result exists.

annotations.label: Absolute value not differentiable at 0

anchor.snippet: the absolute value function given by

annotations.note: `not_differentiableAt_abs_zero` states `¬ DifferentiableAt ℝ abs 0`.

annotations.label: Function with vertical tangent

anchor.snippet: Even a function with a smooth graph is not differentiable at a point where its tangent is vertical

annotations.note: No named example of a vertical-tangent non-differentiability (e.g. cube root) is recorded in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Weierstrass function

anchor.snippet: Weierstrass found the first example of a function that is continuous everywhere but differentiable nowhere

annotations.note: The Weierstrass nowhere-differentiable function is not constructed in Mathlib (only the Weierstrass approximation theorem appears).

annotations.label: Banach's meager set theorem

anchor.snippet: Stefan Banach proved that the set of functions that have a derivative at some point is a meager set

annotations.note: Banach's meagerness result on differentiable functions in `C[0,1]` is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Leibniz notation

anchor.snippet: One common way of writing the derivative of a function is Leibniz notation

annotations.note: Mathlib uses `deriv`/`fderiv` notation, not Leibniz `dy/dx`; no `dy/dx` notation is set up.

annotations.label: Chain rule (Leibniz form)

anchor.snippet: The derivative of a composed function can be expressed using the chain rule

annotations.note: The chain rule itself is `fderiv_comp` / `HasDerivAt.comp`; only the Leibniz notational form is unformalized.

annotations.label: Prime (Lagrange) notation

anchor.snippet: Another common notation for differentiation is by using the prime mark

annotations.note: Mathlib does not introduce `f'` notation for derivatives — it writes `deriv f` instead.

annotations.label: Newton's dot notation

anchor.snippet: In Newton's notation or the dot notation, a dot is placed over a symbol

annotations.note: No dot notation for time derivatives is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: D-notation (Euler)

anchor.snippet: Another notation is D-notation

annotations.note: Mathlib does not provide Euler's `D` operator notation.

annotations.label: Derivative of powers

anchor.snippet: Derivatives of powers

annotations.note: `deriv_pow_field` gives `deriv (· ^ n) x = n * x^(n-1)`.

annotations.label: Derivatives of exponentials and logarithms

anchor.snippet: Functions of exponential , natural logarithm , and logarithm with general base

annotations.note: `Real.deriv_exp` and `Real.deriv_log` give the derivatives of exp and log; general-base versions are in the SpecialFunctions library.

annotations.label: Derivatives of trigonometric functions

anchor.snippet: Trigonometric functions

annotations.note: `Real.deriv_sin = cos` and `Real.deriv_cos = -sin` (and tan via quotient) are established.

annotations.label: Derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions

anchor.snippet: Inverse trigonometric functions

annotations.note: `Real.deriv_arcsin` and `Real.deriv_arctan` give the standard inverse-trig derivative formulas.

annotations.label: Constant rule

anchor.snippet: Constant rule

annotations.note: `fderiv_const c` proves the derivative of a constant function is zero.

annotations.label: Sum rule

anchor.snippet: Sum rule

annotations.note: `fderiv_add` states `fderiv (f + g) = fderiv f + fderiv g` under differentiability hypotheses.

annotations.label: Product rule

anchor.snippet: Product rule

annotations.note: `deriv_mul` / `fderiv_mul` give the Leibniz product rule.

annotations.label: Quotient rule

anchor.snippet: Quotient rule

annotations.note: `deriv_div` proves the quotient rule when the denominator is nonzero.

annotations.label: Chain rule

anchor.snippet: Chain rule for composite functions

annotations.note: `fderiv_comp` (and `HasDerivAt.comp`) is the chain rule for composition.

annotations.label: Computation example

anchor.snippet: The derivative of the function given by

annotations.note: A worked-out specific derivative example like this is not stored as a named lemma in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: The derivative of the function given by

annotations.label: Antiderivative

anchor.snippet: An antiderivative of a function

annotations.note: `HasDerivAt F f x` expresses that `F` is an antiderivative of `f` at `x`, but no dedicated `Antiderivative` notion is defined.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of calculus

anchor.snippet: The fundamental theorem of calculus shows that finding an antiderivative

annotations.note: `integral_eq_sub_of_hasDerivAt` is the FTC: `∫ x in a..b, f' x = f b - f a`.

annotations.label: Higher-order derivatives

anchor.snippet: Higher-order derivatives are the result of differentiating a function repeatedly

annotations.note: `iteratedDeriv n f` and `iteratedFDeriv` formalize n-fold differentiation.

annotations.label: Differentiability class C^k

anchor.snippet: then the function is said to be of differentiability class

annotations.note: `ContDiff 𝕜 n f` is the C^k class (with n : ℕ∞ω).

annotations.label: Smooth (infinitely differentiable) function

anchor.snippet: A function that has infinitely many derivatives is called infinitely differentiable or smooth

annotations.note: Smoothness is `ContDiff 𝕜 ∞ f` (or `ContDiff 𝕜 ω f` for real-analytic).

annotations.label: Polynomials are smooth

anchor.snippet: Any polynomial function is infinitely differentiable

annotations.note: `CPolynomialOn.contDiffOn` shows continuously polynomial functions are `C^∞`; a more elementary `Polynomial`-level statement is also available via `Polynomial.contDiff` lemmas.

annotations.label: Position, velocity, acceleration, jerk

anchor.snippet: represents an object's position with respect to time

annotations.note: Kinematic interpretation (position/velocity/acceleration/jerk) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Derivative of a vector-valued function

anchor.snippet: The derivative of [MATH] is defined to be the vector

annotations.note: `deriv : (𝕜 → F) → 𝕜 → F` already takes values in an arbitrary normed space `F`, covering vector-valued curves.

anchors.snippet: The derivative of [MATH] is defined to be the vector

annotations.label: Partial derivative

anchor.snippet: A partial derivative of a function of several variables is its derivative with respect to one of those variables

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated `partialDeriv`; partials are obtained as `lineDeriv 𝕜 f x eᵢ` along basis vectors or via `fderiv` composed with coordinate inclusions.

annotations.label: Partial derivative in a direction

anchor.snippet: In general, the partial derivative of a function

annotations.note: Subsumed by `lineDeriv`/`HasLineDerivAt`, but no dedicated notion of partial derivative along a coordinate axis exists by name.

anchors.snippet: In general, the partial derivative of a function

annotations.label: Gradient

anchor.snippet: which is called the gradient of

annotations.note: `gradient f x` (notation `∇ f x`) is defined via the Fréchet derivative and inner product duality.

anchors.snippet: which is called the gradient of

annotations.label: Directional derivative

anchor.snippet: then the directional derivative of [MATH] in the direction of

annotations.note: `lineDeriv 𝕜 f x v` is exactly the directional derivative of `f` at `x` along `v`.

anchors.snippet: then the directional derivative of [MATH] in the direction of

annotations.label: Directional derivative formula

anchor.snippet: then they determine the directional derivative of

annotations.note: The formula `fderiv 𝕜 f x v = lineDeriv 𝕜 f x v` is recorded via `HasFDerivAt.hasLineDerivAt` and related lemmas.

anchors.snippet: then they determine the directional derivative of

annotations.label: Total derivative

anchor.snippet: The total derivative of [MATH] at [MATH] is the unique linear transformation

annotations.note: `fderiv 𝕜 f x : E →L[𝕜] F` is precisely the (unique) total/Fréchet derivative.

anchors.snippet: The total derivative of [MATH] at [MATH] is the unique linear transformation

annotations.label: Pushforward

anchor.snippet: is called the pushforward of

annotations.note: `mfderiv I I' f x` (and `tangentMap`) is the pushforward of tangent vectors by a differentiable map between manifolds.

annotations.label: Total derivative determines partials and directional derivatives

anchor.snippet: then all the partial derivatives and directional derivatives of

annotations.note: From `HasFDerivAt f f' x` one obtains `HasLineDerivAt 𝕜 f (f' v) x v` for every direction `v`.

anchors.snippet: then all the partial derivatives and directional derivatives of

annotations.label: Jacobian matrix

anchor.snippet: This matrix is called the Jacobian matrix of

annotations.note: No `jacobian` matrix is named for the Fréchet derivative; one can take the matrix representation of `fderiv` in coordinates, and `MeasureTheory.Function.Jacobian` uses its determinant for change-of-variables.

anchors.snippet: This matrix is called the Jacobian matrix of

annotations.label: Complex derivative

anchor.snippet: The notion of the derivative of such a function is obtained by replacing real variables with complex variables

annotations.note: `deriv` and `fderiv` are parametric in the scalar field; taking `𝕜 = ℂ` yields the complex (holomorphic) derivative.

annotations.label: Cauchy–Riemann equations

anchor.snippet: the complex derivative only exists if the real derivative is complex linear

annotations.note: This theorem characterizes complex differentiability as real differentiability plus the CR-type condition `fderiv ℝ f s x I = I • fderiv ℝ f s x 1`.

annotations.label: Derivative on manifolds

anchor.snippet: The derivative (or differential) of a (differentiable) map

annotations.note: `mfderiv I I' f x` is the differential of a map between smooth manifolds between tangent spaces.

annotations.label: Gateaux and Fréchet derivatives

anchor.snippet: those generalizations are the Gateaux derivative and the Fréchet derivative

annotations.note: `fderiv` is the Fréchet derivative; `lineDeriv` plays the role of the Gateaux/directional derivative.

annotations.label: Weak derivative

anchor.snippet: a concept known as the weak derivative

annotations.note: Sobolev/weak derivatives of distributions are not formalized in Mathlib (only tempered distributions and related infrastructure exist).

annotations.label: Arithmetic derivative

anchor.snippet: The arithmetic derivative involves the function that is defined for the integers by the prime factorization

annotations.note: The number-theoretic arithmetic derivative on `ℕ` defined via prime factorization is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Derivations

anchor.snippet: Derivations generalize derivatives to algebraic settings

annotations.note: `Derivation R A M` is the algebraic notion of an `R`-linear map satisfying the Leibniz rule.
